text.title: Kubernetes Secrets Integration
text.summary: External Secrets Operator setup, Vault Secrets Operator, IRSA and Workload Identity auth, and secure injection patterns.

rule.statement: Use External Secrets Operator (ESO) as the standard Kubernetes integration layer — it supports all major backends (AWS SM, GCP SM, Azure KV, Vault) with a unified API.
rule.rationale: Native Kubernetes Secrets are base64-encoded, not encrypted at rest by default. ESO syncs from an encrypted backend and recreates the K8s Secret automatically on rotation, without restarting pods.

example.code: # ClusterSecretStore — backend connection apiVersion: external-secrets.io/v1beta1 kind: ClusterSecretStore metadata: name: aws-secrets-manager spec: provider: aws: service: SecretsManager region: us-east-1 auth: jwt: serviceAccountRef: name: external-secrets-sa namespace: external-secrets --- # ExternalSecret — specific secret sync apiVersion: external-secrets.io/v1beta1 kind: ExternalSecret metadata: name: db-credentials namespace: production spec: refreshInterval: 1h secretStoreRef: name: aws-secrets-manager kind: ClusterSecretStore target: name: db-credentials creationPolicy: Owner data: - secretKey: password remoteRef: key: prod/app/db property: password

rule.statement: On AWS EKS, use IRSA (IAM Roles for Service Accounts) to bind a Kubernetes ServiceAccount to an AWS IAM role — no AWS credentials in pods or Secrets.
rule.rationale: IRSA uses OIDC token exchange: the pod presents a K8s JWT, AWS STS exchanges it for temporary credentials scoped to the IAM role. Credentials auto-rotate every hour.

example.code: apiVersion: v1 kind: ServiceAccount metadata: name: app-sa namespace: production annotations: eks.amazonaws.com/role-arn: arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/app-prod-role
example.note: The IAM role trust policy must allow sts:AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity from the OIDC provider and restrict the sub claim to the specific namespace:serviceaccount.

section.title: Security Hardening

rule.statement: Enable envelope encryption for etcd using a KMS provider key — base64 encoding in native Secrets is not encryption.
rule.rationale: Without envelope encryption, anyone with etcd access (backup files, etcd snapshots) can read all Kubernetes Secrets in plaintext after a simple base64 decode.

rule.statement: Never log secrets: ensure application code masks credential values in logs and that Kubernetes audit logs do not capture Secret data.
rule.rationale: Secrets in logs are effectively unencrypted and distributed — logs go to ELK/Loki, S3, and potentially third-party log aggregators.

antipattern.description: Storing secrets in ConfigMaps because they are easier to manage.
antipattern.reason: ConfigMaps have no access control differentiation from regular config. Any pod that can read ConfigMaps in a namespace can read all secrets stored there.

section.reference: Vault policy template with least-privilege path scoping
